China used to account for over 20% of Nvidia’s data center revenue, and though there has been hope in the last quarter that this could be salvaged with the right trade deals and products, it is now pretty much official that China is no longer an Nvidia fan.

As much was said by Jensen Huang in a recent interview with Citadel Securities.

We went from 95% market share to 0%, and so I can’t imagine any policymaker thinking that that’s a good idea, that whatever policy we implemented caused America to lose one of the largest markets in the world,

Source: TechRadar.

The good news is that, in theory, the current guidance was already not taking into account any potential revenue from China.

Current estimates have Nvidia at $54.66 billion for Q3. This is still a healthy 17% QoQ growth.

Assuming around 80-85 % of total revenue comes from Data Centers, then we’d be looking at $45 billion for the segment.

This would equate to QoQ growth of 9% which, to be fair, would be a re-acceleration compared to Q2. However, we must also consider that Q3 has usually been a seasonally strong quarter for Nvidia.

Is Competition A Problem?

Okay, the China thing may be priced in. But what about the recent increase in competitive alternatives to Nvidia?

As I highlighted in my most recent Nvidia article, companies like Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) are beginning to catch up in terms of performance.

The MI450 threatens to take market share from the Blackwell.

But of course, Nvidia is not going to sit idly by and let competitors take their market.

In this regard, we should expect to see a lot of talk about the next levels of the AI revolution.

Specifically, I’d like to update on NVLink Fusion, Nvidia’s recently launched custom silicone which enables Nvidia chips to be used with others in order to create custom AI data centers.

Beyond that, I/m sure there will be plenty of talk on the outlook for robotics and even quantum.

We, in fact, have the Nvidia GTC coming up in Washington D.C, which will give us a taste of what comes next.

Is AI A Bubble?

Lastly, I’m sure the AI bubble notion will be addressed at some point. Since Nvidia’s last earnings, there has been a mountain of deals which many have deemed to be circular and irresponsible.

Not only do I want to hear Jensen talk about the deals Nvidia has made, but also his outlook on companies like OpenAI (OPENAI) and the deals they have struck with companies like AMD and Oracle (ORCL).

As has been pointed out recently here on Seeking Alpha, Nvidia has a very robust and growing portfolio of AI ventures.

But this could become a double-edged sword if AI valuations peak.

Jensen should lay out a clear strategy of what the company is seeking to achieve with these investments.

Buying The Earnings Dip?

How does this all translate into earnings?

Here’s what I want to know:

  • Have Nvidia’s margins peaked given the increased competition?

  • Does the company have any hope of recovering China revenues?

  • Any new insights on Rubin’s successor, Feynman?

  • And with all the deals already in place to expand capacity, is there really room for more?

Hopefully Jensen will provide some clarity here.

Ultimately, though, I think there’s a pretty nice set-up for a selloff. Earnings so far have faced increased scrutiny from investors.

Netflix (NFLX) plummeted 10%, Tesla (TSLA) is down after earnings, and the broader stock market has been quite volatile in the last few weeks.

With all this uncertainty, valuations being still high, and technicals overstretched, a post earnings pullback would be very natural.

We have a bearish MACD crossover that just took place, and that generally leads to some weakness.

The 50 week EMA sits at $150, and I wouldn't be surprised to see Nvidia test somewhere close to those levels in coming weeks.

At that price, Nvidia would be more attractive, and I’d consider buying depending on what I see from the latest earnings.

Final Thoughts

Nvidia is the gold standard of AI, which makes their earnings so much more important. If Nvidia disappoints, then this could actually lead to a chain reaction that could be very bearish for AI stocks.

However, I’m still very excited to see what surprises Nvidia and Jensen have in store for us.
